summary:
Trench warfare defined the Western Front of [[event:110|World War I]] from late 1914 until the final Allied offensives of 1918. Following the [[event:3322|Battle of the Marne]], both sides dug defensive positions that evolved into a system of elaborate trenches stretching over 700 kilometers from the Swiss border to the North Sea. The trenches were organized in multiple lines — front trenches, support trenches, reserve trenches — connected by communication trenches and protected by barbed wire and machine gun positions. Life in the trenches was characterized by mud, rats, lice, disease, constant artillery bombardment, and periodic assault operations that often produced catastrophic casualties for minimal territorial gain.

The technological and tactical conditions of 1914–1918 favored defense over offense. Machine guns, artillery, and barbed wire could decimate attacking infantry crossing open ground before they reached enemy positions. Telephone communications allowed defenders to move reserves to threatened points faster than attackers could exploit breakthroughs. New weapons including poison gas (first used by Germany at Ypres in April 1915), tanks (introduced by Britain in 1916), and aircraft were developed as attempts to break the stalemate, with mixed results.

The human cost was staggering. The [[event:2641|Battle of the Somme]] and the [[event:2642|Battle of Verdun]], both fought in 1916, each produced casualties exceeding one million. The psychological trauma of trench warfare — the random death of artillery, the fear of gas attack, the physical squalor — produced a generation marked by what was called shell shock (today recognized as post-traumatic stress disorder) and shaped European literature, art, and political culture for generations.
